project.title: Astral-DIA Proteomics Deciphers the Molecular Landscape of Human Spermatozoa: A Comprehensive Quantitative Proteomic Characterization of Sperm Function and Fertility Potential
project.projectDescription: Male infertility is fundamentally rooted in developmental defects of germ cells and associated molecular dysregulation, yet the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. Data-independent acquisition (DIA) has emerged as a powerful tool in discovery proteomics, enabling the identification of disease biomarkers, therapeutic targets, and molecular pathways with high precision and reproducibility. To analyze the aberrant regulatory events in human sperm proteins associated with male reproductive disorders in a high-throughput, reproducible, and reliable manner, we employed the Orbitrap Astral mass spectrometer, which independently operates Orbitrap Full Scan and Astral MS/MS to generate high-resolution full-scan spectra and high-quality secondary maps. Leveraging the principles of data-independent acquisition (DIA) technology, we constructed the most comprehensive human sperm proteomic expression profile reported to date, encompassing 10,464 proteins, 200,951 precursors, 156,670 modified peptides, and 147,879 peptides.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Optimized protein extraction using urea buffer combined with ultrasound (UA-ultrasound) enabled the identification of over 8,000 proteins per mass spectrometry injection, achieving unprecedented depth and coverage. Our findings reveal potential mechanisms underlying the loss of sperm motility in asthenozoospermia and highlight aberrant regulatory events in human sperm proteins that may critically influence sperm quality and reproductive potential. This study not only establishes a robust proteomic framework for understanding male infertility but also provides novel insights into the molecular basis of sperm dysfunction, offering valuable resources for the development of diagnostic biomarkers and therapeutic strategies in reproductive medicine.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: This study was conducted with approval from the Reproductive Medicine Ethics Committee of Peking University Third Hospital (approval numbers: [insert numbers]). A total of 47 human sperm samples were collected, including 24 from normal controls and 23 from patients with asthenozoospermia, following informed consent. Detailed sample information is provided in Supplementary Table 1. Total protein lysates were extracted from sperm samples using T-PER and urea-assisted (UA) ultrasound methods, followed by tryptic digestion into peptides. Basic reversed-phase chromatography was employed to fractionate the digested peptides, generating 47 raw mass spectrometry data files
keywords: Astral-dia; proteomics; human spermatozoa; sperm function
